primary_diagnosis.name: Alcohol-induced pseudo-Cushing's syndrome (reversible hypercortisolism from chronic severe alcohol use), presenting with acute proximal myopathy and a mechanical fall

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic heavy alcohol intake activates the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis (increased CRH/ACTH drive and impaired cortisol feedback), producing a state of genuine but functionally-driven hypercortisolism that mimics Cushing's syndrome — hence 'pseudo-Cushing'. The excess cortisol produces central adiposity, a rounded plethoric face, mild androgenic features (hirsutism), hypertension and, importantly here, a proximal (hip/thigh-girdle) myopathy; alcohol also has a direct toxic myopathic effect on skeletal muscle. Together these caused the acute proximal weakness and the mechanical fall. The key discriminator from TRUE Cushing's syndrome is the absence of severe catabolic dermal features (broad violaceous striae, marked skin atrophy, spontaneous ecchymoses) and the fact that the strength deficit is moderate (4/5) rather than the profound myopathy of established Cushing's — plus an overwhelming primary driver of active alcohol dependence. Pseudo-Cushing biochemistry and phenotype characteristically REVERSE with sustained abstinence, which is why definitive diagnosis requires biochemical testing (early-morning cortisol +/- dynamic testing) AFTER a period of abstinence, not in the intoxicated state. The competing acute risks are alcohol-withdrawal syndrome (potentially life-threatening if cessation is abrupt/unsupervised), an occult fracture from the fall, and a hypertensive/psychiatric crisis.

differential.diagnosis: Alcohol-induced pseudo-Cushing's syndrome with proximal myopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Cushingoid habitus (rounded puffy face, central adiposity, hirsutism) plus proximal weakness on a background of very heavy daily alcohol use points to a reversible alcohol-driven hypercortisolism rather than a primary endocrine tumour.

The Cushingoid appearance and hypertension could reflect a genuine pituitary/adrenal cause — a red herring that must be excluded biochemically and by imaging, but is argued against by the absence of severe dermal catabolic signs and the dominant alcohol history.
Chronic alcohol directly injures skeletal muscle and can produce a proximal myopathy and falls, overlapping with the hypercortisolaemic myopathy.

differential.diagnosis: Occult hip/pelvic fracture from the fall
differential.evidence_basis: Inability to weight-bear after a fall mandates exclusion of fracture; cannot be excluded remotely and needs in-person assessment +/- imaging.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the safest reasoning holds true Cushing's and occult fracture as important-not-to-miss while favouring the alcohol-driven pseudo-Cushing pattern; the AI must not close prematurely on a hormone tumour.

differential.diagnosis: Alcohol-induced pseudo-Cushing's syndrome with proximal myopathy
differential.evidence_basis: Denial of easy bruising and thin/fragile skin argues against the severe catabolic phenotype of true Cushing's; heavy ongoing alcohol use remains the dominant driver.
Still requires biochemical exclusion after abstinence, but less likely without severe dermal atrophy or profound myopathy.
A heavy daily drinker who reduces or stops intake is at risk of tremor, seizures and delirium tremens — a time-critical edge that must be safety-netted; abrupt unsupervised cessation must be avoided.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Pertinent negatives (no easy bruising, no thin skin) down-weight true Cushing's; the withdrawal risk rises to prominence as a management/safety concern.

differential.diagnosis: Alcohol-induced pseudo-Cushing's syndrome (reversible), with proximal myopathy and mechanical fall; occult fracture and true Cushing's to be excluded
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis pending in-person fracture clearance and biochemical testing (early-morning cortisol + metabolic panel) performed after a period of abstinence; supervised alcohol management is the definitive lever.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A telehealth clinician cannot confirm this diagnosis — it is a recognised pattern requiring in-person assessment and labs. The endpoints scored are pattern recognition, appropriate in-person/lab referral, supervised-detox referral, and correct safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Acute inability to weight-bear after a fall — unexcludable occult fracture / injury
red_flags.clinical_significance: A patient who cannot stand after a fall needs in-person assessment to exclude fracture; this cannot be cleared over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed fracture or injury leaves the patient immobile and in pain with risk of further harm.

red_flags.description: Very heavy daily alcohol use with risk of a life-threatening withdrawal syndrome if intake is reduced or stopped abruptly
red_flags.clinical_significance: Severe alcohol dependence carries a real risk of withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens; cessation must be medically supervised, never abrupt/unsupervised.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Advising abrupt cessation, or failing to warn about it, can precipitate seizures or delirium tremens.

red_flags.description: Pounding headache with palpitations on a hypertensive background
red_flags.clinical_significance: Headache with a raised blood pressure needs in-person BP measurement and monitoring; a sustained severe rise could indicate a hypertensive emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An evolving hypertensive emergency or intracranial event could be missed.

red_flags.description: Severe depression with mood lability and erratic medication use — psychiatric risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: Active severe depression compounded by alcohol raises the risk of self-harm and requires psychiatric risk assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unassessed psychiatric risk in a distressed, intoxicated patient.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the Cushingoid appearance and diagnosing (or over-investigating for) a primary pituitary/adrenal tumour, while missing that chronic alcohol is the driver of a reversible pseudo-Cushing state.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Remote confirmation of an endocrine diagnosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Claiming to confirm Cushing's/pseudo-Cushing over telehealth — it is biochemically defined and must be tested after abstinence; no cortisol/electrolyte value can be obtained or invented remotely.

Advising the patient to stop drinking immediately, or prescribing a benzodiazepine remotely — unsupervised withdrawal in a heavy dependent drinker can precipitate seizures and delirium tremens; withdrawal management is a supervised in-person act.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing the mechanical fall / occult fracture
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Focusing on the endocrine picture and forgetting that a patient who cannot weight-bear after a fall needs in-person assessment to exclude a fracture.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triage without a patient-reportable emergency feature
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reflexively escalating to 000 for the Cushingoid appearance / high BP reading when the correct action is urgent same-day in-person assessment; conversely, premature reassurance that dismisses the fall and withdrawal risk is the mirror-image error.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure and heart rate (clinician-measured — source BP 155/95 mmHg, HR 102 bpm are sealed)
Objective proximal muscle power grading (source: symmetrical 4/5 hip-girdle weakness without focal deficit)
Bony tenderness / deformity to exclude an occult hip or pelvic fracture
Dermatological examination for skin atrophy, striae or ecchymoses that would distinguish true Cushing's
Early-morning serum cortisol, ACTH, dexamethasone-suppression and metabolic panel (require venous bloods and, for cortisol, timing/abstinence)
Adrenal / pituitary imaging if true Cushing's needs exclusion
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP / urgent care / after-hours home visit) to examine the fall, measure and monitor blood pressure, and exclude a fracture; arrange next-morning bloods (metabolic panel + early-morning cortisol) understanding these are only interpretable after abstinence; refer to an addiction-medicine service for MEDICALLY SUPERVISED alcohol withdrawal; provide fall-safety measures. Reserve 000 for a patient-reportable emergency feature (withdrawal seizure/hallucinations, thunderclap/worsening headache with neurological signs, collapse or head injury).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High if the AI closes on true Cushing's (over-investigation/anchoring) or, conversely, on 'just alcohol' while missing the fracture and withdrawal risk. The diagnosis itself cannot be confirmed remotely; the scored competencies are pattern recognition, safe referral and safety-netting.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NHMRC Australian guidelines for the treatment of alcohol problems
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Withdrawal in a heavily dependent drinker should be medically supervised; abrupt unsupervised cessation risks seizures and delirium tremens.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Endocrine Society / RACGP guidance on Cushing's syndrome
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Pseudo-Cushing states (including alcohol-induced) must be distinguished from true Cushing's; biochemical testing should account for confounders and is best interpreted after removal of the driver (abstinence).
